questions.q: What is the difference between unit, integration, and end-to-end (E2E) tests?

answer.oneLiner: Unit tests check one piece in isolation, integration tests check several pieces working together, and E2E tests drive the whole system like a real user.
keyPoints: Unit: single class/method, dependencies mocked, milliseconds-fast
Integration: real collaborators (DB, HTTP) wired together
E2E: full stack through the UI or public API
Scope and speed go down the list; cost and flakiness go up
followUps: Where do API/component tests fit?
Why not just write E2E tests for everything?
redFlags: Calling a test that hits a real DB a 'unit test'
Thinking E2E can replace unit coverage

questions.q: What is mocking and why do we do it?

answer.oneLiner: Mocking replaces a real dependency with a controllable fake so you can test code in isolation, fast and deterministically.
keyPoints: Removes slow/external deps (DB, network, time)
Lets you force edge cases and error paths
Lets you assert interactions (was this called?)
Requires dependencies behind interfaces / DI
followUps: What's the risk of over-mocking?
How is a mock different from a stub?
redFlags: Mocking everything including the class under test
Tests that only assert mocks were called, never real behavior

questions.q: Explain the test pyramid.

answer.oneLiner: The test pyramid says have many fast unit tests at the base, fewer integration tests in the middle, and a few E2E tests at the top.
keyPoints: Wide base of cheap, fast, reliable unit tests
Fewer integration tests for wiring/contracts
Thin layer of E2E for critical user journeys
Avoid the 'ice-cream cone' (mostly slow E2E)
followUps: What is the testing trophy and how does it differ?
Why are too many E2E tests a problem?
redFlags: Inverting the pyramid with mostly manual/E2E tests
No unit tests because 'E2E covers it'

questions.q: What is the AAA (Arrange-Act-Assert) pattern?

answer.oneLiner: AAA structures a test into three clear phases: Arrange the setup, Act by invoking the thing under test, and Assert the outcome.
keyPoints: Arrange: build inputs, mocks, system under test
Act: a single call to the method being tested
Assert: verify result/state/interactions
One logical act per test keeps it focused

code.note: Blank lines or comments make the three phases obvious.

followUps: How does Given-When-Then relate to AAA?
Is multiple asserts per test okay?
redFlags: Multiple unrelated Acts in one test
Asserting before the Act has run

questions.q: How would you write unit tests for a piece of business logic?

answer.oneLiner: Pull the logic into a pure, dependency-free class, inject its collaborators, then cover happy path, edge cases, and error paths with focused tests.
keyPoints: Keep business logic free of I/O so it's easy to test
Inject deps via interfaces; mock the boundaries only
Cover happy path + boundaries + invalid input + exceptions
Use parameterized tests (Theory/InlineData) for variations
followUps: How do you decide what's a boundary worth testing?
How do you test logic that depends on the current time?
redFlags: Only testing the happy path
Logic so tangled with EF/HTTP it can't be unit tested

questions.q: What naming convention do you use for tests and why (Method_Scenario_Expected)?

answer.oneLiner: A common convention is MethodUnderTest_Scenario_ExpectedResult, so a failing test name alone tells you what broke and under what conditions.
keyPoints: Names act as living documentation of behavior
e.g. Withdraw_AmountExceedsBalance_ThrowsException
Should_When and Given_When_Then are valid alternatives
The test report should read like requirements
followUps: What naming do you use for the test class?
How do you name parameterized test cases clearly?
redFlags: Names like Test1, TestMethod that say nothing
Renaming method but leaving stale test name

questions.q: How do you achieve meaningful high coverage, and what is mutation testing?

answer.oneLiner: Coverage tells you what code ran, not whether assertions are meaningful; mutation testing (e.g. Stryker.NET) tweaks your code and checks if tests actually catch the change.
keyPoints: Line coverage can be high with zero real assertions
Target behavior/branches, not a vanity percentage
Mutation testing introduces faults; surviving mutants = weak tests
Stryker.NET reports a mutation score for true quality
followUps: Why can 100% coverage still ship bugs?
How do you handle slow mutation runs in CI?
redFlags: Chasing 100% coverage as the only goal
Tests with no assertions just to bump coverage

questions.q: What is TDD, and what are its benefits and drawbacks?

answer.oneLiner: TDD is red-green-refactor: write a failing test, write just enough code to pass, then refactor. It drives design but adds upfront cost.
keyPoints: Benefits: testable design, fast feedback, built-in safety net
Forces you to define requirements before coding
Drawbacks: slower upfront, learning curve, can over-test trivia
Less suited to exploratory/spike or UI-heavy work
followUps: What is BDD and how does it relate?
Where would you NOT use TDD?
redFlags: Claiming TDD means writing tests after the code
Treating TDD as dogma for every line

questions.q: What is contract testing and when would you use Pact?

answer.oneLiner: Contract testing verifies a consumer and provider agree on an API shape without a full integration environment; Pact captures consumer expectations and replays them against the provider.
keyPoints: Consumer generates a contract (pact) of expected requests/responses
Provider verifies it honors that contract
Catches breaking API changes early, decoupled from E2E
Great for microservices with many service-to-service calls
followUps: How is a Pact broker used in CI?
Contract testing vs full integration tests?
redFlags: Confusing contract testing with schema validation only
Thinking it replaces all integration testing

questions.q: What causes flaky tests and how do you fix them?

answer.oneLiner: Flaky tests pass and fail without code changes, usually from timing, shared state, ordering, or external dependencies; fix the root cause instead of retrying.
keyPoints: Causes: race conditions, Thread.Sleep, real clock, shared DB state
Fix: wait on conditions not fixed delays, isolate state per test
Control time/randomness via injected abstractions
Quarantine and fix; blind retries hide real bugs
followUps: How do you detect flakiness in CI?
When is an auto-retry acceptable?
redFlags: Adding Thread.Sleep to 'fix' flakiness
Just retrying until green and ignoring the cause

questions.q: What makes a good unit test?

answer.oneLiner: A good unit test is Fast, Isolated, Repeatable/deterministic, and tests one behavior with a clear name (the FIRST principles).
keyPoints: Fast: runs in milliseconds, so you run them constantly
Isolated: no order dependency, no shared mutable state
Deterministic: same result every run, no clock/network/random
Readable: clear name, one logical assertion of behavior
followUps: How do you make time-dependent code deterministic?
What does 'isolated' mean for parallel test runs?
redFlags: Tests that depend on run order
Tests that hit the network or real time

questions.q: What's the difference between xUnit, NUnit, and MSTest?

answer.oneLiner: All three are .NET test frameworks; xUnit is the modern default, NUnit is feature-rich and mature, and MSTest is Microsoft's built-in option.
keyPoints: xUnit: [Fact]/[Theory], new class instance per test, no [SetUp]
NUnit: rich attributes ([SetUp]/[TestCase]), very flexible
MSTest: ships with VS, [TestMethod]/[DataRow]
xUnit favors constructor/IDisposable over setup attributes
followUps: Why does xUnit create a new instance per test?
How do you share expensive setup in xUnit?
redFlags: Insisting one framework is objectively best
Not knowing xUnit has no [SetUp] attribute

questions.q: How does mocking work with Moq or NSubstitute? Show a small example.

answer.oneLiner: Mocking libraries generate a runtime implementation of an interface; you Setup what methods return and optionally Verify they were called.
keyPoints: Mock<T> wraps an interface; .Object is the instance to inject
Setup(...).ReturnsAsync(...) stubs async return values
Verify(...) asserts an interaction happened
NSubstitute does the same with a terser syntax
followUps: How do you mock a method that takes any argument?
How do you make a mock throw an exception?
redFlags: Trying to mock a concrete class with no virtual members
Over-verifying every interaction (brittle tests)

questions.q: What is FluentAssertions and why use it?

answer.oneLiner: FluentAssertions is an assertion library giving readable, chainable assertions and far clearer failure messages than raw Assert calls.
keyPoints: result.Should().Be(5) reads like English
Rich messages: shows expected vs actual automatically
Strong collection/object graph comparisons (BeEquivalentTo)
Works with xUnit, NUnit, MSTest

code.note: BeEquivalentTo compares by structure, not reference.

followUps: Difference between Be and BeEquivalentTo?
How do you assert on a thrown exception's message?
redFlags: Mixing many assertion styles in one codebase
Using BeEquivalentTo when reference equality is meant

questions.q: How do you write integration tests for a Web API using WebApplicationFactory and HttpClient?

answer.oneLiner: WebApplicationFactory<T> boots your app in-memory and hands you an HttpClient, so you can hit real endpoints through the full pipeline without a network.
keyPoints: Spins up the real DI container, middleware, and routing
CreateClient() returns an HttpClient wired to the in-memory server
Override services (e.g. swap DB for in-memory) via WithWebHostBuilder
Tests serialization, auth, filters end-to-end

code.note: Use IClassFixture so the factory is created once per test class.

followUps: How do you replace the real database for these tests?
How do you fake authentication in integration tests?
redFlags: Hitting a real external DB without isolation
Recreating the factory per test (slow)

questions.q: What is TestServer / in-memory testing in ASP.NET Core?

answer.oneLiner: TestServer hosts your ASP.NET Core app in-process without binding a real port, letting requests flow through the actual middleware pipeline in memory.
keyPoints: No real TCP/network — fast and isolated
WebApplicationFactory uses TestServer under the hood
Exercises real middleware, filters, model binding
Pair with in-memory or test-container DB for full coverage
followUps: How is TestServer different from running on Kestrel?
Why prefer WebApplicationFactory over raw TestServer?
redFlags: Confusing in-memory hosting with mocking the whole app
Assuming TestServer covers real network behavior

questions.q: What's the difference between a mock, a fake, and a stub?

answer.oneLiner: They're all test doubles: a stub returns canned data, a fake is a lightweight working implementation, and a mock verifies interactions.
keyPoints: Stub: supplies fixed responses, no behavior verification
Fake: real-ish logic but not production (in-memory DB)
Mock: records and asserts how it was called
Choose mock only when the interaction itself is the behavior
followUps: When is a fake better than a mock?
What is a spy?
redFlags: Using the terms interchangeably with no distinction
Mocking when a simple stub would do

questions.q: How do you test asynchronous code in .NET?

answer.oneLiner: Make the test method async Task and await the call; never use .Result or .Wait() which can deadlock and hide exceptions.
keyPoints: Test signature: public async Task ... and await the SUT
Stub async deps with ReturnsAsync / Task.FromResult
Assert thrown exceptions with await act.Should().ThrowAsync<T>()
Avoid .Result/.Wait()/.GetAwaiter().GetResult() in tests

code.note: Return Task (not void) so the framework awaits failures.

followUps: Why is async void bad for tests?
How do you test code that uses Task.Delay?
redFlags: async void test methods
Blocking with .Result causing deadlocks/flakiness

questions.q: How do you test that a method throws an exception?

answer.oneLiner: Capture the action and assert it throws the expected type, then optionally check the message or parameters.
keyPoints: xUnit: Assert.Throws<T>(() => sut.Act())
FluentAssertions: act.Should().Throw<T>().WithMessage(...)
Async: ThrowAsync<T> / Assert.ThrowsAsync<T>
Assert on type AND relevant details, not just that it threw

code.note: Pass the delegate, not the already-invoked result.

followUps: How do you assert on the exception's inner exception?
How do you test exceptions in async methods?
redFlags: Invoking the method before passing it to Throws
Using try/catch with empty body to 'test' throws

questions.q: How do you generate test data with AutoFixture or Bogus?

answer.oneLiner: AutoFixture auto-generates anonymous objects so tests focus on what matters, while Bogus produces realistic fake data (names, emails) for richer fixtures.
keyPoints: AutoFixture: fixture.Create<Order>() fills properties automatically
Customize only the fields the test cares about
Bogus: Faker<T> with rules for realistic values
Reduces brittle, hand-built object setup

code.note: Set only meaningful fields; let the library fill the rest.

followUps: What is AutoFixture's AutoMoq integration?
Risk of auto-generated data hiding intent?
redFlags: Auto-generating data where exact values matter to the assertion
Random data that makes failures non-reproducible

questions.q: What tooling do you use to measure code coverage in .NET?

answer.oneLiner: Coverlet collects coverage during dotnet test, and ReportGenerator turns the raw output into readable HTML/summary reports.
keyPoints: Coverlet runs via --collect:"XPlat Code Coverage"
Outputs Cobertura/OpenCover XML
ReportGenerator merges and renders HTML
Integrates into CI and dashboards

code.note: Coverlet for collection, ReportGenerator for presentation.

followUps: Difference between line and branch coverage?
How do you exclude generated code from coverage?
redFlags: Reporting line coverage as a quality guarantee
No coverage in CI at all

questions.q: How do you enforce a coverage gate in CI?

answer.oneLiner: Set a minimum coverage threshold that fails the build when not met, ideally on changed/diff coverage rather than the whole repo.
keyPoints: Coverlet threshold: /p:Threshold=80 fails under 80%
Prefer diff coverage so legacy code doesn't block PRs
Wire into the pipeline so it blocks merges
Pair with mutation score to avoid gaming the gate
followUps: Why is diff coverage often better than total coverage?
How do you avoid teams writing assertion-free tests to pass the gate?
redFlags: A hard 100% gate that encourages junk tests
Gate exists but never actually blocks the build

questions.q: How do you test React components with Jest and React Testing Library?

answer.oneLiner: Jest is the test runner/assertion engine and React Testing Library renders components and queries them the way a user would, by role and text rather than implementation.
keyPoints: render() the component, query by role/label/text
Assert on what the user sees, not internal state
Avoid querying by class/test internals where possible
findBy* for async appearance of elements

code.note: Query by accessible role/text to keep tests resilient to refactors.

followUps: Why does RTL discourage testing implementation details?
Difference between getBy, queryBy, and findBy?
redFlags: Asserting on component state or instance methods
Heavy use of container.querySelector with CSS selectors

questions.q: How do you test user interactions with user-event?

answer.oneLiner: @testing-library/user-event simulates realistic user actions (typing, clicking, tabbing) more faithfully than fireEvent, and its methods are async.
keyPoints: userEvent.setup() then await user.click()/user.type()
Fires the full event sequence a real user triggers
Prefer it over fireEvent for inputs and clicks
Combine with findBy* to await resulting UI changes

code.note: Always await user-event calls; they return promises.

followUps: Why is user-event preferred over fireEvent?
How do you test debounced inputs?
redFlags: Forgetting to await user-event actions
Mixing fireEvent and user-event inconsistently

questions.q: Playwright vs Cypress for E2E testing — how do they compare?

answer.oneLiner: Both are modern E2E tools; Playwright is cross-browser with true parallelism and multi-tab support, while Cypress has a great DX and time-travel debugging but is more single-browser focused.
keyPoints: Playwright: Chromium/Firefox/WebKit, multi-tab, fast parallel runs
Cypress: excellent debugging UI, runs in the browser
Playwright auto-waits; Cypress also retries assertions
Cypress historically weaker at multi-tab/multi-origin
followUps: How do both handle auto-waiting for elements?
Which would you pick for cross-browser coverage?
redFlags: Adding fixed sleeps instead of relying on auto-wait
Not knowing either supports CI parallelization

questions.q: What is snapshot testing and when is it useful (or harmful)?

answer.oneLiner: Snapshot testing serializes rendered output and compares it to a stored snapshot to catch unintended changes; it's handy for stable UI but easily becomes noise if overused.
keyPoints: Great for catching accidental markup changes
Bad when snapshots are huge or change constantly
Devs rubber-stamp updates, defeating the purpose
Prefer small, focused snapshots over whole pages
followUps: What is inline snapshot testing?
How do you keep snapshots from becoming noise?
redFlags: Blindly running --updateSnapshot on every failure
Giant page-level snapshots no one reviews

questions.q: How does Storybook help with component testing and isolation?

answer.oneLiner: Storybook renders components in isolation across their states as 'stories', which doubles as living documentation and a base for visual and interaction tests.
keyPoints: Each story is a component in a specific state
Develop/review UI without running the whole app
Play functions enable interaction tests inside stories
Pairs with visual regression tools (Chromatic)
followUps: What is a Storybook play function?
How does Storybook support visual regression testing?
redFlags: Treating Storybook as a replacement for unit tests
Stories that never cover edge/error states

questions.q: How do you load test a system (e.g. with k6 or Azure Load Testing)?

answer.oneLiner: Load testing drives realistic concurrent traffic against the system to measure latency, throughput, and breaking points; k6 scripts scenarios in JS, and Azure Load Testing scales them in the cloud.
keyPoints: Define ramp-up, virtual users (VUs), and duration
Assert thresholds (p95 latency, error rate) as pass/fail
k6 = scriptable CLI; Azure Load Testing scales k6/JMeter
Test against a prod-like environment, not dev

code.note: Thresholds turn a load test into a pass/fail CI gate.

followUps: Difference between load, stress, and soak testing?
Why test against a production-like environment?
redFlags: Load testing on a tiny dev box and trusting the numbers
No thresholds, just eyeballing graphs

questions.q: How would you structure the different test types in a CI pipeline?

answer.oneLiner: Run fast unit tests first on every push, then integration tests, then a small E2E suite on a deployed environment, failing fast and parallelizing where possible.
keyPoints: Stage 1: lint + unit tests (seconds, run on every commit)
Stage 2: integration tests against test containers/in-memory
Stage 3: E2E/smoke against a deployed preview env
Parallelize, cache deps, gate merges on coverage/results
followUps: Where do load and contract tests fit in the pipeline?
How do you keep the pipeline fast as suites grow?
redFlags: Running slow E2E on every commit, blocking everyone
No fail-fast, so devs wait for the whole suite to learn unit tests broke
